docs.title: Stride Language
docs.text:
Stride is a statically typed, JIT-compiled language using the LLVM compiler toolchain.

This project implements a compiler for the Stride programming language, along with a web-based code editor featuring syntax highlighting.

Currently, it only supports primitive types, though support for structs, classes, and generics is planned.

Structs are already partially implemented, though member access is not yet implemented.

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
cstride/
└── packages/
    ├── compiler/
    │   └── ... (compiler source files)
    ├── standard-library/
    │   └── ... (standard library files)
    └── editor/
        └── ... (web-based editor files)

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
Contributions are welcome!

Please open issues or submit pull requests for improvements or bug fixes.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Follow these instructions to build and run the Stride compiler and editor.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
1. CMake >= 3.1
  2. Node.js >= 24.0
  3. LLVM 21.1.8

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
You can install these packages using the following commands:

=== "macOS"

```shell
brew install cmake node@24 llvm@21
```

=== "Ubuntu/Debian"

```shell
sudo apt install cmake nodejs llvm-21
```

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository: 
    git clone https://github.com/VxTi/cstride.git
cd cstride
    The packages/ directory contains the compiler and editor source code.

docs.title: Building the Compiler
docs.text:
Run the following commands from the project root directory: 

cd packages/compiler
cmake -S . -B cmake-build-debug
cmake --build cmake-build-debug --target cstride

Optionally: Add the binary to your $PATH, if you wish to use it directly.

docs.title: Running the Compiler
docs.text:
From the packages/compiler directory, you can either run it directly: 

cstride -c <file1> <file2> ...
Replace <file> with your Stride source file.

Note: This assumes you have the binary in your $PATH, or are in the build directory.

For additional commands, run cstride --help.

docs.title: Using the Editor
docs.text:
The editor is a web-based IDE for Stride, built with Monaco Editor.

  1. Navigate to the editor directory and install dependencies: 
    cd packages/editor
npm install
  2. Start the development server: 
    npm start
  3. Open the provided local URL in your browser (likely http://localhost:3000/).

docs.title: Standard Library
docs.text:
Stride's standard library is located in packages/standard-library/ and includes math, memory, and I/O modules.

This is still in development.

docs.title: Modules
docs.text:
• Math: Basic mathematical functions.
  • Memory: Memory management utilities.
  • I/O: Input and output operations.

docs.title: Using the Standard Library
docs.text:
Imports are currently being implemented. The planned format is:

import std::{ Math };

docs.title: Functions
docs.text: Functions are declared using the fn keyword.

docs.title: Declaration and Invocation
docs.text:
Functions are declared with a name, a list of parameters, and a return type.

fn add(a: i32, b: i32): i32 {
    return a + b
}

// Invoking a function
const result: i32 = add(10, 5)

docs.title: External Functions
docs.text:
You can also create externally linked functions if they are defined in another library using the extern keyword.

extern fn some_c_function(x: i32): i32;

docs.title: Structs
docs.text: Structs are still in development, but they allow for grouping related data.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
struct Point {
    x: i32,
    y: i32,
}

docs.title: Initialization
docs.text: const p = Point::{ x: 10, y: 20 }

docs.title: Type Aliasing with Structs
docs.text:
You can create structs that reference other structs. These function as separate types and will cause a type error if used inappropriately.

struct Vector2d = Point

const p2: Vector2d = Vector2d::{ x: 5, y: 15 } // This is valid
const p3: Point = p2 // This is invalid

docs.title: Variables and Types
docs.text: Stride is a statically typed language.

docs.title: Creating variables
docs.text:
Variables are created using const for constants and let for mutable variables.

// Creating a mutable 32 bit int variable
const x: i32 = 10

// Creating a constant variable
let y: f64 = 3.0D

docs.title: Integer Types
docs.text: Stride supports several integer types: - Signed: i8, i16, i32, i64 - Unsigned: u8, u16, u32, u64

docs.title: Integer Literals
docs.text:
Stride supports various integer literal formats, including decimal and hexadecimal. For example, 0x12345 is a hexadecimal integer literal. 64-bit integer literals can be suffixed with an 'L': 

let large_int: i64 = 12345678901234567890L

docs.title: Floating Point Types
docs.text:
• f32: Single precision
  • f64: Double precision

Floating point literals can be suffixed with 'F' for single precision or 'D' for double precision. For example, 3.14F is a single precision float, and 2.71828D is a double precision float.

docs.title: Other Types
docs.text:
• bool: Boolean type (true, false)
  • char: Character type
  • string: String type
  • void: Void type

docs.title: Operators
docs.text:
Stride uses traditional arithmetic and comparison operators.

let x: i32 = 10
let y: f64 = 3.0D

// Arithmetic operators
let sum: f64 = x + y
let difference: f64 = x - y
let product: f64 = x * y
let quotient: f64 = x / y
let remainder: f64 = x % y

// Comparison operators
let is_equal: bool = x == y
let is_not_equal: bool = x != y
let is_less_than: bool = x < y
let is_greater_than: bool = x > y
let is_less_than_or_equal: bool = x <= y
let is_greater_than_or_equal: bool = x >= y
